text: The surgical team joins the operation after a diagnostic laparoscopy has been completed, with all robotic ports visibly placed in the abdominal area.
The robotic surgical system is docked, and the robotic instruments are positioned and targeted towards the surgical site.
The surgeon performs a laparoscopic intraoperative ultrasound to visualize and identify the anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The video shows the proximity between the celiac artery and the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) during the procedure.
The surgeon begins the operation by using monopolar cautery to open the pars flaccida, a thin membrane located in the upper abdomen.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum overlying the cruciate diaphragm using monopolar cautery, revealing underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon separates the muscle fibers, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection until the anterior surface of the aorta is exposed.
The surgeon completes a significant dissection to reveal the anterior surface of the aorta, visible beneath the robust crura.
The suction device, partially visible off-screen, provides posterior and caudal traction during the procedure.
The surgeon manipulates the tissue to straighten the celiac axis, facilitating the dissection process.
With traction applied, fibrous structures are visibly decussating across the anterior surface of the aorta.
The surgeon identifies and divides the celiac plexus and numerous nerve fibers within the dissection plane on the anterior surface of the aorta.
The surgeon carefully teases out fibrous structures across the anterior surface of the aorta, identifying nerve fibers within the dissection plane.
The surgeon carefully separates components in the area, mindful of veins crossing the same region that could complicate the procedure if divided unnecessarily.
The surgical team has divided a significant amount of tissue overlying the anterior surface of the aorta, but the appearance suggests that a full release has not yet been completed.
The surgeon injects indocyanine green, which is visible in the surgical field.
The aorta is visible, fluorescing due to the injection of endocyanine green, highlighting its location and structure.
The surgical field shows the absence of a fluorescent signal from the celiac artery, contrasting with the previously fluorescing aorta.
The surgeon identifies additional tissue overlying the aorta that must be divided, as the camera detects fluorescence only to a depth of two millimeters, indicating incomplete tissue release.
The surgical team continues the tissue release over the aorta, guided by the additional visual information provided by the fluorescence imaging.
The surgeon divides the neural tissue overlying the celiac plexus, aiming to achieve symptomatic relief by addressing the neurogenic origin.
The surgeon continues the dissection, dividing the decussating fibers crossing the anterior surface of the celiac artery.
The surgical field reveals a significant amount of nerve and inflammatory tissue surrounding the celiac artery, making the dissection process visibly tedious.
The surgeon divides fibers at the base of the celiac artery, which is identified at its origin from the aorta.
The surgeon continues the dissection distally along the celiac artery after identifying its origin, carefully separating surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies additional nerve tissue crossing the anterior surface of the celiac artery, along with visible signs of chronic inflammation in the surrounding area.
The surgeon prepares to re-inject endocyanine green, a fluorescent dye, into the surgical field.
The liver fluoresces brightly as the endocyanine green accumulates in the liver tissue, highlighting its excretion process.
The vasculature is fluorescing, revealing the entire aorta and celiac artery down to the trifurcation, highlighted by the endocyanine green.
The median arcuate ligament appears to be completely divided, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon confirms the presence of nerve tissue crossing the anterior surface of the celiac artery and signs of chronic inflammation, using fluorescence imaging.
The intraoperative ultrasound image shows the celiac artery at the top of the screen, with reduced tissue coverage and a straighter course as it emerges from the aorta.
